mnemonics: Most Everything is Default, Hire Professionals
keyPoints: A default argument value is assigned to a function parameter if no argument is provided when the function is called.
Function parameters can have default values, which allows for more flexibility in function definitions.
If a function parameter has no default value, all calls to that function must provide a value for that parameter.
By default, a function parameter can be used as many times as needed without recompilation.
In C++, default argument values can be used for all types of parameters (e.g., int, char, bool, etc.).
When using default argument values, the order of parameters matters. All non-default parameters must come before any default parameters.
If a default argument value is used for a parameter with no default value, the function will be ambiguous and will not compile.
C++17 introduced deduced default argument values, making the syntax more concise and readable.
